reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors propose a different formulation for a graph neural network, focused on achieving accuracy equal to anisotropic GNNs without anisotropic mechanisms.
reviews.main_review:
This paper is a mixed bag. On one hand, I appreciate the authors' vision: take some things we've learned about GNNs (heads, aggregators), revisit first principles (isotropic GNNs), try to come up with something new that blends them. In addition, I appreciate their take on computational efficiency: they correctly seize upon the notion that a well-understood algorithmic kernel can have meaningful computational impact, and they build on its advantages (e.g., section 3.2-aggregator fusion). On the other hand, many of the conclusions leave something to be desired. The analyses and descriptions sometimes gloss over important factors, and I wish the results delivered a stronger takeaway to match the ambition. For all the talk about computation and memory efficiency, the actual numbers are not all that substantial.

Strengths:

- The authors' direct challenge to accepted folk knowledge about anisotropic GNNs is healthy and welcome. As models evolve over time, it is important for the community to revisit fundamental positions on model components. The paper opens a lot of areas for other researchers to build off it, which I see as a major strength.
- The proposed model is clearly explained and fairly easy to follow. The intrepretations are useful and provide good intuition for how to place their proposed model in the context of other current work.
- The appendices actually provide a lot of useful content, sometimes more than the paper itself. The rationale in appendix C, for instance, was far clearer than the paper's 'aggregator fusion' section in 3.2 (even without the algorithm).

Weaknesses:

- Some of the evaluation results are weak. The accuracy-normalized results in seem to demonstrate that EGC-S  is not all that much faster or smaller than GCN. It also seems to indicate that parameter-normalized results do not correlate particularly well with memory footprint across models, which weakens the argument for O(V) vs. O(E).
- Despite a nominally primary argument of the paper being about memory efficiency and computational performance, very little quantitative results support that argument. I agree that there is definitely a lot of potential for this approach to be efficient, but I wish the paper had strong support for it.

- The authors fail to disambiguate different types of memory effects. For instance, while high-watermark memory footprint (which is mostly what is measured in their experiments) is important, they overlook the effects of parallelism. This leads them, amongst other things, to dismiss sampling methods almost out of hand. The memory behavior of sampled methods is vastly different than non-sampled GNNs when dealing with distributed execution or on graphs that are substantially larger than single-device memory capacity.  Or the focus on O(V)/O(E), which glosses over the fact the average degree is small (between 2 and 13 on their chosen OGB datasets) and ignores other 'constants' on the same order. This is technically correct but misleading. None of these invalidate the authors' methods, but it's somewhat disingenuous.

Also, it very much felt like parts of the paper were aimed at different goals. The intro and background lean heavily into hardware efficiency, while other parts of the paper (S3 outside of the last paragraph; S5.1-5.3) seem to ignore it.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The authors take a different tact with their proposed GNN, which is welcome. They explain it clearly, and the paper's writing and organization are solid. The results support their claim that they can achieve competitive accuracy, but the quantitative computation and memory results are somewhat underwhelming. In my view, the contribution of this paper is its approach and challenge to convention, which feels good enough to publish, even if it left me wanting more substantive.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose a different formulation for a graph neural network, focused on achieving accuracy equal to anisotropic GNNs without anisotropic mechanisms.

Main Review:
This paper is a mixed bag. On one hand, I appreciate the authors' vision: take some things we've learned about GNNs (heads, aggregators), revisit first principles (isotropic GNNs), try to come up with something new that blends them. In addition, I appreciate their take on computational efficiency: they correctly seize upon the notion that a well-understood algorithmic kernel can have meaningful computational impact, and they build on its advantages (e.g., section 3.2-aggregator fusion). On the other hand, many of the conclusions leave something to be desired. The analyses and descriptions sometimes gloss over important factors, and I wish the results delivered a stronger takeaway to match the ambition. For all the talk about computation and memory efficiency, the actual numbers are not all that substantial.

Strengths:

- The authors' direct challenge to accepted folk knowledge about anisotropic GNNs is healthy and welcome. As models evolve over time, it is important for the community to revisit fundamental positions on model components. The paper opens a lot of areas for other researchers to build off it, which I see as a major strength.
- The proposed model is clearly explained and fairly easy to follow. The intrepretations are useful and provide good intuition for how to place their proposed model in the context of other current work.
- The appendices actually provide a lot of useful content, sometimes more than the paper itself. The rationale in appendix C, for instance, was far clearer than the paper's 'aggregator fusion' section in 3.2 (even without the algorithm).

Weaknesses:

- Some of the evaluation results are weak. The accuracy-normalized results in seem to demonstrate that EGC-S  is not all that much faster or smaller than GCN. It also seems to indicate that parameter-normalized results do not correlate particularly well with memory footprint across models, which weakens the argument for O(V) vs. O(E).
- Despite a nominally primary argument of the paper being about memory efficiency and computational performance, very little quantitative results support that argument. I agree that there is definitely a lot of potential for this approach to be efficient, but I wish the paper had strong support for it.

- The authors fail to disambiguate different types of memory effects. For instance, while high-watermark memory footprint (which is mostly what is measured in their experiments) is important, they overlook the effects of parallelism. This leads them, amongst other things, to dismiss sampling methods almost out of hand. The memory behavior of sampled methods is vastly different than non-sampled GNNs when dealing with distributed execution or on graphs that are substantially larger than single-device memory capacity.  Or the focus on O(V)/O(E), which glosses over the fact the average degree is small (between 2 and 13 on their chosen OGB datasets) and ignores other 'constants' on the same order. This is technically correct but misleading. None of these invalidate the authors' methods, but it's somewhat disingenuous.

Also, it very much felt like parts of the paper were aimed at different goals. The intro and background lean heavily into hardware efficiency, while other parts of the paper (S3 outside of the last paragraph; S5.1-5.3) seem to ignore it.

Summary Of The Review:
The authors take a different tact with their proposed GNN, which is welcome. They explain it clearly, and the paper's writing and organization are solid. The results support their claim that they can achieve competitive accuracy, but the quantitative computation and memory results are somewhat underwhelming. In my view, the contribution of this paper is its approach and challenge to convention, which feels good enough to publish, even if it left me wanting more substantive.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose a different formulation for a graph neural network, focused on achieving accuracy equal to anisotropic GNNs without anisotropic mechanisms.

## Main Review:

This paper is a mixed bag. On one hand, I appreciate the authors' vision: take some things we've learned about GNNs (heads, aggregators), revisit first principles (isotropic GNNs), try to come up with something new that blends them. In addition, I appreciate their take on computational efficiency: they correctly seize upon the notion that a well-understood algorithmic kernel can have meaningful computational impact, and they build on its advantages (e.g., section 3.2-aggregator fusion). On the other hand, many of the conclusions leave something to be desired. The analyses and descriptions sometimes gloss over important factors, and I wish the results delivered a stronger takeaway to match the ambition. For all the talk about computation and memory efficiency, the actual numbers are not all that substantial.

## Strengths:

- The authors' direct challenge to accepted folk knowledge about anisotropic GNNs is healthy and welcome. As models evolve over time, it is important for the community to revisit fundamental positions on model components. The paper opens a lot of areas for other researchers to build off it, which I see as a major strength. For instance, the authors' proposal of EGC raises interesting questions about the role of anisotropy in GNNs and encourages further exploration of isotropic models.
- The proposed model is clearly explained and fairly easy to follow. The interpretations are useful and provide good intuition for how to place their proposed model in the context of other current work. Specifically, the authors provide a clear spatial interpretation of their EGC-S layer, highlighting its node-wise weight matrices, and a spectral interpretation, relating it to localized spectral filtering.
- The appendices actually provide a lot of useful content, sometimes more than the paper itself. The rationale in appendix C, for instance, was far clearer than the paper's 'aggregator fusion' section in 3.2 (even without the algorithm). The authors' detailed experiments and ablation studies, such as the study on the number of heads and bases in section 5.3, provide valuable insights into the design choices of EGC.

## Weaknesses:

- Some of the evaluation results are weak. The accuracy-normalized results in seem to demonstrate that EGC-S is not all that much faster or smaller than GCN. It also seems to indicate that parameter-normalized results do not correlate particularly well with memory footprint across models, which weakens the argument for O(V) vs. O(E). For example, the authors mention that EGC-S has a similar memory footprint to GCN, despite being more accurate. This raises questions about the effectiveness of EGC-S in achieving better efficiency.
- Despite a nominally primary argument of the paper being about memory efficiency and computational performance, very little quantitative results support that argument. I agree that there is definitely a lot of potential for this approach to be efficient, but I wish the paper had strong support for it. The authors provide some latency and memory statistics for parameter-normalized models in Table 4, but these results are limited and do not fully demonstrate the efficiency benefits of EGC.
- The authors fail to disambiguate different types of memory effects. For instance, while high-watermark memory footprint (which is mostly what is measured in their experiments) is important, they overlook the effects of parallelism. This leads them, amongst other things, to dismiss sampling methods almost out of hand. The memory behavior of sampled methods is vastly different than non-sampled GNNs when dealing with distributed execution or on graphs that are substantially larger than single-device memory capacity.  Or the focus on O(V)/O(E), which glosses over the fact the average degree is small (between 2 and 13 on their chosen OGB datasets) and ignores other 'constants' on the same order. This is technically correct but misleading. None of these invalidate the authors' methods, but it's somewhat disingenuous.

## Additional Observations:

- The authors' claim that EGC is a drop-in replacement for existing GNN layers is intriguing, but it would be more convincing with more thorough experiments and analysis. For instance, the authors could have explored the applicability of EGC to different domains, such as computer vision or natural language processing.
- The paper could benefit from a more detailed comparison with other efficient GNN architectures, such as GraphSAGE or SIGN. This would help to better understand the advantages and disadvantages of EGC compared to other approaches.
- The authors mention that EGC can be combined with other approaches for improving efficiency, such as quantization and pruning. However, it would be interesting to see more concrete results on how these combinations could work in practice.

## Summary Of The Review:
The authors take a different tact with their proposed GNN, which is welcome. They explain it clearly, and the paper's writing and organization are solid. The results support their claim that they can achieve competitive accuracy, but the quantitative computation and memory results are somewhat underwhelming. In my view, the contribution of this paper is its approach and challenge to convention, which feels good enough to publish, even if it left me wanting more substantive.

## Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

The expanded review preserves all the original critique points, weaknesses, and strengths, while adding new sentences and paragraphs that elaborate on existing points or add related observations. The review still supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation, and the tone and level of detail remain unchanged.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper claimed they designed a new GNN architecture that achieves state-of-the-art performance with lower memory consumption and latency. More specifically, the proposed model uses memory proportional to the number of vertices in the graph $O(V)$, in contrast to competing methods which require memory proportional to the number of edges $O(E)$. The paper claimed that the new architecture enabled each vertex to have its own weight matrix, thus following a novel adaptive filtering approach. The experiments found that the proposed efficient model could achieve higher accuracy than competing approaches across six large and varied datasets against strong baselines. Moreover, the experiments demonstrated that the proposed method achieves lower latency and memory consumption for the same accuracy compared to competing approaches.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:
1. This paper applied the idea of "basis weights" and thus effectively uses different weight matrices for different nodes.
2. This paper has provided a long section to help interpret the proposed architecture and compared it with GCN, GAT, and PNA.

### Questions:
1. Since it is noted that different aggregators might result in outputs with different means and variances, for the experiments in Table 1, did you normalize the combination weights $w$ or the different aggregated outputs?
2. I am curious how you implemented the "weighting of bases" per node, i.e., multiplication between the combination weighting coefficients $w$ (different for different nodes) and the aggregated outputs corresponding to different basis weight matrices? Is this step before or after the aggregation? Although the theoretical complexity of this step is $O(V)$, I think the choice of actual implementation might significantly affect the practical time efficiency.

### Weaknesses:
1. The major weakness of this paper, in my opinion, is that this paper seems to have misunderstood the real bottleneck to scale-up GNNs, and the proposed architecture may not be suitable for large graphs (i.e., size over a million).
   - The paper has compared with many existing approaches to solve the scalability issues of GNNs on large graphs. However, this paper did not tackle this problem. Firstly, the theoretical complexities of the proposed architecture are still $O(V)$. Thus on a graph with more than a million nodes (which happens in some node classification or link prediction benchmarks), the memory consumption is still too large for conventional GPUs. Secondly, the main experiments (Table 1) are conducted on four graph regression/classification benchmarks and only one node classification benchmark. However, the graphs in the four graph-level benchmarks are small. The experimental exploration and evidence on large graphs are insufficient.
   - For graph sampling approaches, the paper claimed that `We evaluated a variety of sampling approaches and observed that even modest sampling levels, which provide little benefit to memory or latency, cause model performance to decline noticeably.` The corresponding experimental results are shown in Table 8 in the appendix. However, all four benchmarks used in Table 8 are graph-level tasks (graph regression or classification), where the sizes of input graphs are small. For example, ZINC and MolHIV are molecule graphs, and CIFAR consists of super-pixel sampled graphs whose sizes are at most 150 (Dwivedi et al., 2020). Applying sampling strategies to these small input graphs is not reasonable, and it is not strange to observe significant performance degradation. Actually, the performance of some sampling algorithms on relatively large graphs for node classification is strong, e.g., GraphSAINT (SAGE aggregator) can outperform the "Full-batch" GraphSAGE on *ogbn-products* (see [leaderboard](https://ogb.stanford.edu/docs/leader_nodeprop/)). The quoted sentence above is not appropriate.
   - To sum up, I think the architecture proposed in the paper does not solve the scalability problem on large graphs. Thus the authors should make this point clearer in the related work and experiment sections.
2. Another potential problem, in my opinion, is the spectral interpretation in Section 4.2. The paper claims that `Our approach corresponds to learning multiple filters and computing a linear combination of the resulting filters with weights depending on the attributes of each node locally.` The paper compared two formulas: (1) the ideal linear combination of filters: $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot g_{\theta_b}(\mathbf{L})\mathbf{X}$; and (2) the filtering of proposed model (correspond to EGC-S, Eq.(2)) $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot (\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})\mathbf{X}\Theta_b$.  However, I think the latter formula is not a combination of multiple filters. There is only one filter $(\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})$ (which is left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$) and the weight matrix $\Theta_b$ right multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ should not change the relative magnitude of signals of different frequencies. It is not the same if changing $g(\mathbf{L})$ in the former formula, where the matrix left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ changes.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper proposed a novel architecture to linearly combine the aggregated outputs using different weight matrices for each node. The model is memory efficient since the memory consumption is $O(V)$ instead of $O(E)$. However, the proposed method did not solve the scalability problem of GNNs when applied to considerably large graphs. And it is unclear when the provided memory efficiency is necessary, and thus the proposed method is the first choice. In terms of performance, the improvements compared with PNA are also not very convincing. The significance of this paper might be limited if the efficiency/performance improvements are marginal. I would encourage the author to explore the theoretical understanding of the proposed architecture further. Currently, section 4 is well-written, but the conclusions are limited and may have some flaws. In general, I could not recommend the current manuscript for acceptance.

reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper claimed they designed a new GNN architecture that achieves state-of-the-art performance with lower memory consumption and latency. More specifically, the proposed model uses memory proportional to the number of vertices in the graph $O(V)$, in contrast to competing methods which require memory proportional to the number of edges $O(E)$. The paper claimed that the new architecture enabled each vertex to have its own weight matrix, thus following a novel adaptive filtering approach. The experiments found that the proposed efficient model could achieve higher accuracy than competing approaches across six large and varied datasets against strong baselines. Moreover, the experiments demonstrated that the proposed method achieves lower latency and memory consumption for the same accuracy compared to competing approaches.

Main Review:
### Strengths:
1. This paper applied the idea of "basis weights" and thus effectively uses different weight matrices for different nodes.
2. This paper has provided a long section to help interpret the proposed architecture and compared it with GCN, GAT, and PNA.

### Questions:
1. Since it is noted that different aggregators might result in outputs with different means and variances, for the experiments in Table 1, did you normalize the combination weights $w$ or the different aggregated outputs?
2. I am curious how you implemented the "weighting of bases" per node, i.e., multiplication between the combination weighting coefficients $w$ (different for different nodes) and the aggregated outputs corresponding to different basis weight matrices? Is this step before or after the aggregation? Although the theoretical complexity of this step is $O(V)$, I think the choice of actual implementation might significantly affect the practical time efficiency.

### Weaknesses:
1. The major weakness of this paper, in my opinion, is that this paper seems to have misunderstood the real bottleneck to scale-up GNNs, and the proposed architecture may not be suitable for large graphs (i.e., size over a million).
   - The paper has compared with many existing approaches to solve the scalability issues of GNNs on large graphs. However, this paper did not tackle this problem. Firstly, the theoretical complexities of the proposed architecture are still $O(V)$. Thus on a graph with more than a million nodes (which happens in some node classification or link prediction benchmarks), the memory consumption is still too large for conventional GPUs. Secondly, the main experiments (Table 1) are conducted on four graph regression/classification benchmarks and only one node classification benchmark. However, the graphs in the four graph-level benchmarks are small. The experimental exploration and evidence on large graphs are insufficient.
   - For graph sampling approaches, the paper claimed that `We evaluated a variety of sampling approaches and observed that even modest sampling levels, which provide little benefit to memory or latency, cause model performance to decline noticeably.` The corresponding experimental results are shown in Table 8 in the appendix. However, all four benchmarks used in Table 8 are graph-level tasks (graph regression or classification), where the sizes of input graphs are small. For example, ZINC and MolHIV are molecule graphs, and CIFAR consists of super-pixel sampled graphs whose sizes are at most 150 (Dwivedi et al., 2020). Applying sampling strategies to these small input graphs is not reasonable, and it is not strange to observe significant performance degradation. Actually, the performance of some sampling algorithms on relatively large graphs for node classification is strong, e.g., GraphSAINT (SAGE aggregator) can outperform the "Full-batch" GraphSAGE on *ogbn-products* (see [leaderboard](https://ogb.stanford.edu/docs/leader_nodeprop/)). The quoted sentence above is not appropriate.
   - To sum up, I think the architecture proposed in the paper does not solve the scalability problem on large graphs. Thus the authors should make this point clearer in the related work and experiment sections.
2. Another potential problem, in my opinion, is the spectral interpretation in Section 4.2. The paper claims that `Our approach corresponds to learning multiple filters and computing a linear combination of the resulting filters with weights depending on the attributes of each node locally.` The paper compared two formulas: (1) the ideal linear combination of filters: $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot g_{\theta_b}(\mathbf{L})\mathbf{X}$; and (2) the filtering of proposed model (correspond to EGC-S, Eq.(2)) $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot (\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})\mathbf{X}\Theta_b$.  However, I think the latter formula is not a combination of multiple filters. There is only one filter $(\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})$ (which is left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$) and the weight matrix $\Theta_b$ right multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ should not change the relative magnitude of signals of different frequencies. It is not the same if changing $g(\mathbf{L})$ in the former formula, where the matrix left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ changes.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposed a novel architecture to linearly combine the aggregated outputs using different weight matrices for each node. The model is memory efficient since the memory consumption is $O(V)$ instead of $O(E)$. However, the proposed method did not solve the scalability problem of GNNs when applied to considerably large graphs. And it is unclear when the provided memory efficiency is necessary, and thus the proposed method is the first choice. In terms of performance, the improvements compared with PNA are also not very convincing. The significance of this paper might be limited if the efficiency/performance improvements are marginal. I would encourage the author to explore the theoretical understanding of the proposed architecture further. Currently, section 4 is well-written, but the conclusions are limited and may have some flaws. In general, I could not recommend the current manuscript for acceptance.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough

title: Distributionally Robust Fair Principal Components via Geodesic Descents

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers the fair PCA with distribution ally robust optimization algorithm. The problem is of great interest. The key idea of the proposed method is to consider a penalized loss function with the penalty on the fairness criterion. The distributionally robust optimization and its reformulation is developed for parameter estimation.
reviews.main_review:
The strength of the paper is the rigorous optimization framework to solve the proposed fair PCA, with a nice reformulation and theoretical property. 

The weakness of this paper is in the following folds. First, it is not very clear on the significance of the novelty. The authors cast the PCA problem as the minimization of the penalized reconstructed error. Such a formulation is widely common in the machine learning community. Because of the L2-type loss function, the use of the distributionally robust optimization is not that surprising with the known complication on the penalty part. Second, the proposed method involves the selection of tuning parameter. While the authors seem to avoid the discussion tuning parameter selection. Moreover, the numerical experiments just consider the use of fixed value of these tuning parameters, which makes reader hard to judge the quality of the numerical comparison. Third, due the use of the penalty to engage the fairness, it is not how good the proposed method enables the fairness, especially there is no criterion on the selection of the tuning parameter \lambda. Fourth, under the context of fairness, the authors also avoid the discussion on how the choice of k, the number of PCs, would affect the fairness.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Based on the aforementioned strengths and weakness, I would consider the proposed method contain interest merits, with the conservation of the sufficiency on the methodology novelty, framework rigor, and the applicability in practice.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper considers the fair PCA with distribution ally robust optimization algorithm. The problem is of great interest. The key idea of the proposed method is to consider a penalized loss function with the penalty on the fairness criterion. The distributionally robust optimization and its reformulation is developed for parameter estimation.

Main Review:
The strength of the paper is the rigorous optimization framework to solve the proposed fair PCA, with a nice reformulation and theoretical property. 

The weakness of this paper is in the following folds. First, it is not very clear on the significance of the novelty. The authors cast the PCA problem as the minimization of the penalized reconstructed error. Such a formulation is widely common in the machine learning community. Because of the L2-type loss function, the use of the distributionally robust optimization is not that surprising with the known complication on the penalty part. Second, the proposed method involves the selection of tuning parameter. While the authors seem to avoid the discussion tuning parameter selection. Moreover, the numerical experiments just consider the use of fixed value of these tuning parameters, which makes reader hard to judge the quality of the numerical comparison. Third, due the use of the penalty to engage the fairness, it is not how good the proposed method enables the fairness, especially there is no criterion on the selection of the tuning parameter \lambda. Fourth, under the context of fairness, the authors also avoid the discussion on how the choice of k, the number of PCs, would affect the fairness.

Summary Of The Review:
Based on the aforementioned strengths and weakness, I would consider the proposed method contain interest merits, with the conservation of the sufficiency on the methodology novelty, framework rigor, and the applicability in practice.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Summary Of The Paper:
This paper presents a distributionally robust optimization algorithm for fair Principal Component Analysis (PCA). The proposed method addresses the issue of fairness in PCA by incorporating a penalized loss function with a fairness criterion. The authors develop a rigorous optimization framework to solve the proposed fair PCA, including a reformulation and theoretical properties.

## Main Review:

### Strengths
The paper's strength lies in its rigorous optimization framework, which provides a solid foundation for solving the proposed fair PCA problem. The reformulation of the distributionally robust optimization problem is a notable contribution, as it allows for a more tractable and efficient solution.

### Weaknesses
However, the paper has several weaknesses. Firstly, the novelty of the proposed method is not entirely clear. Casting the PCA problem as the minimization of a penalized reconstructed error is a common formulation in the machine learning community. The use of distributionally robust optimization is not surprising, given the L2-type loss function and the known complications with the penalty part.

#### Tuning Parameter Selection
The proposed method involves the selection of tuning parameters, which is not thoroughly discussed in the paper. The authors seem to avoid discussing the tuning parameter selection process, and the numerical experiments only consider fixed values of these tuning parameters. This makes it challenging for the reader to judge the quality of the numerical comparison. For instance, the authors mention that they search for the neighborhood size εa and the penalty parameter λ over specific ranges, but they do not provide a clear justification for these choices.

#### Fairness Criterion
The paper also lacks a clear discussion on how the proposed method enables fairness, especially in the absence of a criterion for selecting the tuning parameter λ. The authors mention that they use a fairness-aware PCA problem formulation, but they do not provide a clear explanation of how this formulation ensures fairness. Furthermore, the paper does not discuss how the choice of k, the number of principal components, affects the fairness of the proposed method.

#### Numerical Experiments
The numerical experiments are limited, and the authors do not provide a comprehensive comparison with other state-of-the-art methods. For example, they only compare their method with FairPCA and CFPCA, but they do not consider other fair PCA methods, such as those proposed by Samadi et al. (2018) and Olfat & Aswani (2019).

## Expanded Review:

### Significance of Novelty
While the paper's proposed method is built upon existing techniques, the authors' contribution lies in their ability to reformulate the fair PCA problem as a distributionally robust optimization problem. This reformulation allows for a more principled approach to handling uncertainty in the data, which is a crucial aspect of fairness in machine learning.

### Tuning Parameter Selection
The authors' decision to fix the tuning parameters in their numerical experiments is understandable, given the complexity of the problem and the need to demonstrate the effectiveness of their method. However, in practice, the selection of these parameters will depend on the specific problem instance and the desired trade-off between fairness and accuracy.

### Fairness Criterion
The authors' use of a fairness-aware PCA problem formulation is a step in the right direction, but a more detailed discussion of the fairness criterion and its implications would be beneficial. Specifically, the authors could discuss how their method ensures fairness in the presence of different types of data distributions and how it compares to other fairness criteria.

### Choice of k
The choice of k, the number of principal components, is a critical aspect of PCA, and its impact on fairness should be explored in more detail. The authors could discuss how different values of k affect the fairness of their method and provide guidance on how to select k in practice.

## Conclusion
In conclusion, while the paper has some weaknesses, it also presents a rigorous optimization framework for solving the proposed fair PCA problem. With some additional discussion and exploration of the tuning parameter selection, fairness criterion, and choice of k, the paper could make a more significant contribution to the field.

## Rating
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

The expanded review preserves the same decision/rating as the original review while providing more detailed feedback and suggestions for improvement. The paper's strengths and weaknesses are clearly outlined, and the reviewer provides guidance on how to address the weaknesses and improve the paper.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the formulation, reformulation and algorithm for distributionally robust fairness-aware PCA. The reformulation exploits techniques from distributionally robust optimization, and the algorithm is based on reimannian sub-gradient descent. The theory is tested on UCI datasets.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

1. The paper is nicely written and easy to follow. The motivation is clear and the problem formulation is novel.

2. Results on reformulation and riemannian gradient descent render tractable algorithms for solving the proposed problem. I do not find major technical errors.

3. Numerical experiments are based on real data set and are comprehensive.

Weakness:

1. The formulation simply combines distributioanlly robust PCA with a fairness penalty. Although this formulation is new, it seems a natural extension of existing frameworks.

2. The theoretical results are relatively easy to derive based on existing results on distributionally robust optimization and riemannian optimization. Hence, the originality is not methodological, but just applying well-established tools to a new formulation.

3. The dimensions of variables in UCI datasets are still relatively low (<=40 according to Table 2). The results can be strengthened by testing on datasets of larger dimensions.

My only reservation is that most theory are relatively straightforward compared to existing literature on distributionally robust optimization and Riemannian optimization.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies the formulation, reformulation and algorithm for distributionally robust fairness-aware PCA. The reformulation exploits techniques from distributionally robust optimization, and the algorithm is based on reimannian sub-gradient descent. The theory is tested on UCI datasets.

Main Review:
Strength:

1. The paper is nicely written and easy to follow. The motivation is clear and the problem formulation is novel.

2. Results on reformulation and riemannian gradient descent render tractable algorithms for solving the proposed problem. I do not find major technical errors.

3. Numerical experiments are based on real data set and are comprehensive.

Weakness:

1. The formulation simply combines distributioanlly robust PCA with a fairness penalty. Although this formulation is new, it seems a natural extension of existing frameworks.

2. The theoretical results are relatively easy to derive based on existing results on distributionally robust optimization and riemannian optimization. Hence, the originality is not methodological, but just applying well-established tools to a new formulation.

3. The dimensions of variables in UCI datasets are still relatively low (<=40 according to Table 2). The results can be strengthened by testing on datasets of larger dimensions.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposes a new formulation for PCA and derives a complete set of results with regard to the computational issues of the new results. My only reservation is that most theory are relatively straightforward compared to existing literature on distributionally robust optimization and Riemannian optimization.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies the formulation, reformulation, and algorithm for distributionally robust fairness-aware PCA. The reformulation exploits techniques from distributionally robust optimization, and the algorithm is based on Riemannian sub-gradient descent. The theory is tested on UCI datasets.

## Main Review:
### Strengths:

1. **Clear Motivation and Novel Problem Formulation**: The paper is nicely written and easy to follow. The motivation is clear, and the problem formulation is novel, effectively combining fairness criteria with distributionally robust optimization for Principal Component Analysis (PCA).

2. **Tractable Algorithms**: The results on reformulation and Riemannian gradient descent provide tractable algorithms for solving the proposed problem. I do not find major technical errors in the derivations.

3. **Comprehensive Numerical Experiments**: The numerical experiments are based on real datasets and are comprehensive, covering various UCI datasets to validate the performance of the proposed method.

### Weaknesses:

1. **Natural Extension of Existing Frameworks**: The formulation simply combines distributionally robust PCA with a fairness penalty. Although this formulation is new, it seems a natural extension of existing frameworks, which might not be considered highly innovative.

2. **Methodological Originality**: The theoretical results are relatively easy to derive based on existing results on distributionally robust optimization and Riemannian optimization. Hence, the originality is not methodological but rather applying well-established tools to a new formulation.

3. **Dimensionality of Datasets**: The dimensions of variables in UCI datasets are still relatively low (<=40 according to Table 2). The results can be strengthened by testing on datasets of larger dimensions, which could provide more insights into the scalability and performance of the proposed method.

## Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposes a new formulation for PCA that incorporates fairness and distributional robustness, derives a complete set of results regarding the computational issues of the new formulation, and provides a comprehensive set of numerical experiments. My only reservation is that most of the theory is relatively straightforward compared to the existing literature on distributionally robust optimization and Riemannian optimization.

## Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

## Expanded Review:

The paper presents a well-structured approach to integrating fairness and robustness into PCA through distributionally robust optimization. The use of Riemannian sub-gradient descent for solving the problem is well-motivated and effectively explained. 

One potential area for further strengthening the paper is in demonstrating the practical impact of the proposed method on real-world applications, especially those with high-dimensional data. While the experiments on UCI datasets provide a good starting point, exploring applications in more complex domains could highlight the benefits of the method more clearly.

Furthermore, the paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion on the choice of hyperparameters, especially the penalty parameter for fairness and the radius of the ambiguity set. The sensitivity of the results to these parameters could provide additional insights into the robustness and applicability of the method.

In terms of methodology, while the application of existing tools to a new problem is valuable, the paper could explore more deeply the implications of the distributionally robust approach on fairness and robustness. For instance, analyzing the trade-offs between fairness, robustness, and reconstruction error could offer more nuanced insights into the performance of the method.

Overall, the paper is well-written, and the proposed method is interesting and relevant. With some additional exploration of its practical implications and methodological nuances, it could have a more significant impact on the field.

The decision to marginally accept the paper is based on its clear presentation, novel formulation, and comprehensive experiments. However, the reservations regarding the originality of the methodology and the dimensionality of the datasets suggest that while the paper is above the acceptance threshold, it is not significantly distinguished by its methodological contributions. Therefore, the rating of 6 is appropriate, reflecting a marginally above-average contribution that warrants publication but also suggests areas for further development.

My only reservation is that most theory are relatively straightforward compared to existing literature on distributionally robust optimization and Riemannian optimization.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper aims to improve fairness and distributional robustness in dimensionality reduction techniques. More specifically, it proposes a regularization term to principal compoenent analysis (PCA) such that the expected reconstruction errors for each groups (conditioned on a binary sensitive variable) are similar, and relaxes constraint on the empirical probability distribution by maximizing over a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set. This is optimized with subgradient descent on the Stiefel manifold, and numerical experiments comparing the proposed method to previous fair PCA are conducted.
reviews.main_review:
This paper tackles an interesting problem, and is mostly well-written and easy to follow. The problem formulation and re-formulation steps are not trivial.

However, there are places in this paper that needs more explanation and justification. 

- There needs to be a discussion on the assumptions for Theorem 3.2. How restrictive are they? Were they satisfied in the experiments? For example, (i) implies that the regularization parameter is less than 0.5, which will restrict the flexibility shown in Figure 1. 
- The ambiguity set B(P) is a central part of the proposed method. What is the justification for using Wasserstein-type divergence as opposed to others? Also, the second line of equation (5) needs to be explained more carefully.
- In defining $gap_\mu(U)$, is $||U'-U||_F$ the best choice to measure the difference between two orthonormal matrices? One can think of many examples with rotations where $U$ and $U'$ span the same subspace but will have very large frobenius norm difference. A more typical choice in the literature would be $||UU^T - U' U' ^T||$. 
- Is the RHS of Theorem 4.3. necessarily decreasing with $t$? It's not obvious to me why this guarantees convergence.
- Experiments and discussions on the robustness of the proposed method are lacking, compared to the fairness aspect. Are there any comparisons to other robust PCA methods, or simulation experiments?
- In the experiments, how were the sensitive attributes chosen? Were they based on what are considered sensitive attributes in the real world, e.g. race, gender, or were they chosen randomly?
- This paper is lacking a conclusion section. It would have made it stronger if the authors mentioned how they would extend this work to non-binary sensitive attributes.
- On a very high level, it's not *as* obvious why reconstruction error should be balanced compared to, for example, in supervised learning. The toy example in Figure 1 is helpful but I'm wondering if the authors can describe a real-world example where bias in reconstruction error will be harmful - where PCA is the end result rather than a preprocessing step for supervised learning problem.

Other minor problems that can be fixed with careful editing, such as:
- Page 2: "signal process" -> signal processing
- Page 5: "By the definition the ambiguity..."
- Page 7: "At each iteration, the point..." sentence is hard to parse.
- Page 9: "In Each dataset, ..." "other features is..." "the the generalization..."

reviews.text:
## Summary Of The Paper:
This paper aims to improve fairness and distributional robustness in dimensionality reduction techniques. More specifically, it proposes a regularization term to principal component analysis (PCA) such that the expected reconstruction errors for each group (conditioned on a binary sensitive variable) are similar, and relaxes the constraint on the empirical probability distribution by maximizing over a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set. This is optimized with subgradient descent on the Stiefel manifold, and numerical experiments comparing the proposed method to previous fair PCA methods are conducted.

## Main Review:
This paper tackles an interesting problem and is mostly well-written and easy to follow. The problem formulation and re-formulation steps are not trivial.

However, there are places in this paper that need more explanation and justification.

- There needs to be a discussion on the assumptions for Theorem 3.2. How restrictive are they? Were they satisfied in the experiments? For example, (i) implies that the regularization parameter is less than 0.5, which will restrict the flexibility shown in Figure 1. The authors should provide more insights into the implications of these assumptions on the performance of the proposed method.

- The ambiguity set B(P) is a central part of the proposed method. What is the justification for using Wasserstein-type divergence as opposed to others? The authors mention that the divergence W coincides with the squared type-2 Wasserstein distance between two Gaussian distributions, but more discussion on the choice of ambiguity set would be helpful. Additionally, the second line of equation (5) needs to be explained more carefully, as it may not be immediately clear to readers.

- In defining $gap_\mu(U)$, is $||U'-U||_F$ the best choice to measure the difference between two orthonormal matrices? One can think of many examples with rotations where $U$ and $U'$ span the same subspace but will have very large Frobenius norm difference. A more typical choice in the literature would be $||UU^T - U' U' ^T||$. The authors should justify their choice of metric and discuss potential implications.

- Is the RHS of Theorem 4.3 necessarily decreasing with $t$? It's not obvious why this guarantees convergence. The authors should provide more details on the convergence guarantee and address potential concerns.

- Experiments and discussions on the robustness of the proposed method are lacking, compared to the fairness aspect. Are there any comparisons to other robust PCA methods, or simulation experiments? The authors should provide more comprehensive evaluations of the proposed method.

- In the experiments, how were the sensitive attributes chosen? Were they based on what are considered sensitive attributes in the real world, e.g., race, gender, or were they chosen randomly? The authors should provide more context on the experimental setup.

- This paper is lacking a conclusion section. It would have made it stronger if the authors mentioned how they would extend this work to non-binary sensitive attributes. The authors should also discuss potential future directions and implications of their work.

- On a very high level, it's not immediately obvious why reconstruction error should be balanced compared to, for example, in supervised learning. The toy example in Figure 1 is helpful, but the authors could describe a real-world example where bias in reconstruction error will be harmful, and PCA is the end result rather than a preprocessing step for a supervised learning problem.

## Expanded Review:

The paper proposes a distributionally robust fairness-aware PCA method that balances the trade-off between the total reconstruction error and the reconstruction error gap between subgroups. The method is formulated as a min-max optimization problem over the Stiefel manifold and is solved using a Riemannian subgradient descent algorithm.

One of the main contributions of the paper is the proposal of a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set, which is used to robustify the PCA method against perturbations in the empirical distribution. The authors provide a detailed explanation of the ambiguity set and its properties, but more discussion on the choice of divergence would be helpful.

The paper also provides a series of experiments to evaluate the performance of the proposed method. However, the experiments are mostly focused on the fairness aspect, and more evaluations on the robustness of the method would be helpful. The authors should provide more comprehensive comparisons to other robust PCA methods and simulation experiments.

In terms of the theoretical results, the paper provides a detailed analysis of the convergence guarantee of the Riemannian subgradient descent algorithm. However, more discussion on the implications of the assumptions for Theorem 3.2 would be helpful. The authors should provide more insights into the implications of these assumptions on the performance of the proposed method.

## Additional Comments:

- Page 2: "signal process" -> signal processing
- Page 5: "By the definition the ambiguity..." could be rephrased for clarity.
- Page 7: "At each iteration, the point..." sentence is hard to parse and could be rephrased.
- Page 9: "In Each dataset, ..." "other features is..." "the the generalization..." could be rephrased for clarity.

## Summary Of The Review:
It's a good paper, but it is missing some interpretation of its theoretical results and justifications. The authors should provide more details on the assumptions, ambiguity set, and convergence guarantee, as well as more comprehensive evaluations of the proposed method.

## Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

The decision/rating remains exactly the same, and the expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation. All original critique points are preserved and remain unchanged, and new sentences and paragraphs are added to elaborate on existing points or add related observations. The expansion uses specific details from the manuscript to support the points, and the review reads naturally and fluently with the new content integrated seamlessly.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper presents an optimization based formulation of fair principal components analysis problem with a particular focus on the robustness of the optimal models. In the case when the desired robustness is specified by Gaussian distributions, the authors show how to adapt a manifold subgradient descent to solve a non-convex reformulation of the proposed fair pca formulation.
reviews.main_review:
Strength: + Robustness criterion for linear transformation of fairness can be formulated as a single optimization problem as long as the ambiguity set is defined using a single normal distribution.
+ A smooth reformulation is presented which can be solved using standard riemannian gradient descent algorithm.

Weakness: - Generalization to multiclass sensitive attribute, and other fairness metrics is missing.
- Figure 1 illustrating different approaches is nice, although it fails to capture the distributional robustness aspect of the formulation presented in the paper.
- It is hard to follow the paper since the notations are not standard.
- Experimental results are preliminary and conclusions are not clear.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Justification:  The technical content is interesting, although in its current form, it is hard to follow. For example, why is problem (4)  infinite dimensional? As far as I can see, the decision variables in optimization problem (4) is a d by k matrix which is finite dimensional, although the zeroth order and first order oracles maybe noisy. This is evident in their convergence analysis, where they can only bound the gradient norm of the reformulated variable U (which in its original formulation is V). The paper contains some technical insights in the formulation as a SDP which happens to have a nice connection to Riemannian geometry as illustrated. However, at times, the paper reads as if one has to refer multiple textbooks just to make sure that paper indeed is technically correct. For example, many quantities appear without definitions (\hat{p}_a is not defined), and sometimes with somewhat unclear notations (Q(dx\times da). To me, the biggest technical question that the paper fails to answer is the generalization to nonbinary features. This is important in the context of the paper's contribution because we know that standard SDP solvers do not scale to settings with big or high dimensional data. The convergence rate in this setting is also not clear since a naive extension would indicate at least polynomial slowdown. The experiments are also inconclusive since the paper focuses exclusively on the binary sensitive attribute setting, and with just one metric. While the approach here may not be directly extendable to other metrics, the paper completely misses on discussing when the chosen formulation is appropriate for practitioners.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper presents an optimization based formulation of fair principal components analysis problem with a particular focus on the robustness of the optimal models. In the case when the desired robustness is specified by Gaussian distributions, the authors show how to adapt a manifold subgradient descent to solve a non-convex reformulation of the proposed fair pca formulation.

Main Review:
Strength: + Robustness criterion for linear transformation of fairness can be formulated as a single optimization problem as long as the ambiguity set is defined using a single normal distribution.
+ A smooth reformulation is presented which can be solved using standard riemannian gradient descent algorithm.

Weakness: - Generalization to multiclass sensitive attribute, and other fairness metrics is missing.
- Figure 1 illustrating different approaches is nice, although it fails to capture the distributional robustness aspect of the formulation presented in the paper.
- It is hard to follow the paper since the notations are not standard.
- Experimental results are preliminary and conclusions are not clear.

Summary Of The Review:
Justification:  The technical content is interesting, although in its current form, it is hard to follow. For example, why is problem (4)  infinite dimensional? As far as I can see, the decision variables in optimization problem (4) is a d by k matrix which is finite dimensional, although the zeroth order and first order oracles maybe noisy. This is evident in their convergence analysis, where they can only bound the gradient norm of the reformulated variable U (which in its original formulation is V). The paper contains some technical insights in the formulation as a SDP which happens to have a nice connection to Riemannian geometry as illustrated. However, at times, the paper reads as if one has to refer multiple textbooks just to make sure that paper indeed is technically correct. For example, many quantities appear without definitions (\hat{p}_a is not defined), and sometimes with somewhat unclear notations (Q(dx\times da). To me, the biggest technical question that the paper fails to answer is the generalization to nonbinary features. This is important in the context of the paper's contribution because we know that standard SDP solvers do not scale to settings with big or high dimensional data. The convergence rate in this setting is also not clear since a naive extension would indicate at least polynomial slowdown. The experiments are also inconclusive since the paper focuses exclusively on the binary sensitive attribute setting, and with just one metric. While the approach here may not be directly extendable to other metrics, the paper completely misses on discussing when the chosen formulation is appropriate for practitioners.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:
The paper presents an optimization-based formulation of the fair principal components analysis problem, with a particular focus on the robustness of the optimal models. In the case when the desired robustness is specified by Gaussian distributions, the authors show how to adapt a manifold subgradient descent to solve a non-convex reformulation of the proposed fair PCA formulation.

Main Review:

Strengths:
+ The paper effectively formulates the robustness criterion for linear transformation of fairness as a single optimization problem, as long as the ambiguity set is defined using a single normal distribution.
+ A smooth reformulation is presented, which can be solved using a standard Riemannian gradient descent algorithm.

Weaknesses:
- The generalization to multiclass sensitive attributes and other fairness metrics is missing, which is a significant limitation of the proposed approach.
- Figure 1 illustrating different approaches is nice, although it fails to capture the distributional robustness aspect of the formulation presented in the paper.
- The paper is hard to follow due to non-standard notations, which can make it challenging for readers to understand the technical details.
- The experimental results are preliminary, and the conclusions are not clear, which makes it difficult to assess the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

Summary of the Review:
Justification: The technical content is interesting, although in its current form, it is hard to follow. For example, why is problem (4) considered infinite-dimensional? As far as I can see, the decision variables in optimization problem (4) are a d by k matrix, which is finite-dimensional, although the zeroth-order and first-order oracles may be noisy. This is evident in their convergence analysis, where they can only bound the gradient norm of the reformulated variable U (which in its original formulation is V). The paper contains some technical insights into the formulation as an SDP, which happens to have a nice connection to Riemannian geometry, as illustrated. However, at times, the paper reads as if one has to refer to multiple textbooks just to make sure that the paper is indeed technically correct. For example, many quantities appear without definitions (\hat{p}_a is not defined), and sometimes with somewhat unclear notations (Q(dx\times da)). To me, the biggest technical question that the paper fails to answer is the generalization to non-binary features. This is important in the context of the paper's contribution because we know that standard SDP solvers do not scale to settings with big or high-dimensional data. The convergence rate in this setting is also not clear since a naive extension would indicate at least polynomial slowdown. The experiments are also inconclusive since the paper focuses exclusively on the binary sensitive attribute setting and with just one metric. While the approach here may not be directly extendable to other metrics, the paper completely misses discussing when the chosen formulation is appropriate for practitioners.

One of the key concerns is that the paper does not provide a clear explanation of how the proposed approach can be extended to handle non-binary sensitive attributes. This is a significant limitation, as many real-world applications involve multiple sensitive attributes. Furthermore, the paper does not discuss the scalability of the proposed approach to large datasets, which is an important consideration in many applications.

The paper also lacks a clear comparison with existing approaches to fair PCA. While the authors mention a few related works, they do not provide a thorough comparison of the proposed approach with these existing methods. This makes it difficult to assess the novelty and effectiveness of the proposed approach.

In terms of the technical presentation, the paper could benefit from more detailed explanations of the technical derivations and proofs. Some of the notation is also non-standard, which can make it challenging to follow the technical details.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

The decision to rate this paper as marginally below the acceptance threshold is based on the following factors:

* The paper presents an interesting and novel approach to fair PCA, which has the potential to be impactful in many applications.
* The technical content is sound, and the authors provide a clear explanation of the proposed approach.
* However, the paper has several limitations, including the lack of generalization to non-binary sensitive attributes, unclear experimental results, and non-standard notation.

To improve the paper, the authors should address the following concerns:

* Provide a clear explanation of how the proposed approach can be extended to handle non-binary sensitive attributes.
* Discuss the scalability of the proposed approach to large datasets.
* Provide a thorough comparison with existing approaches to fair PCA.
* Clarify the technical derivations and proofs.
* Improve the notation and presentation to make the paper more readable.

By addressing these concerns, the authors can strengthen the paper and make it more suitable for publication. However, in its current form, the paper is marginally below the acceptance threshold.

title: Meta Learning Low Rank Covariance Factors for Energy Based Deterministic Uncertainty

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The authors explore the uncertainty calibration problem in meta-learning few-shot classification, which is a relatively new topic that was seldom discussed before. 
To solve the challenges that the few samples in few-shot learning are usually insufficient for providing reliable estimation to the covariance matrices, the authors introduce meta-learning covariance matrices parametrized by a set encoder (implemented as set-transformer). And the authors further introduce scaled energy to parameterize a logit-normal softmax distribution for improving the uncertainty calibration of the softmax classification layer.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
The overall presentation of this paper is both clear and straightforward.

The figures and equations precisely facilitate the reading. 

No noticeable typos were spotted. 

The topic that the authors selected is important and largely overlooked by the research of meta-learning and few-shot learning. The effectiveness of the proposed method is reflected by the quantitative comparisons. 

Weakness:
My major concern to this paper mainly arises from the use of an additional meta-learned set encoder to predict the task-specific covariance. 
It has been shown in previous work that some meta-learners have natural advantages in quantifying uncertainty. 

For example, relying on kernel methods, Gaussian processes based meta-learners [1,2] have demonstrated strong uncertainty calibration without leveraging any auxiliary components. 

Additionally, I might be wrong, but I'm curious that if this class-level covariance can be obtained through aggregating sample-wise information so that an additional set encoder can be removed. 
Please refer to Eq.6 to Eq. 9 of [3] for an example. Although the motivations are different, I believe there is some shared wisdom. 

[1] Deep kernel transfer in gaussian processes for few-shot learning, NeurIPS 2020

[2] Bayesian Few-Shot Classiﬁcation with One-vs-Each Pólya-Gamma Augmented Gaussian Processes, ICLR 2021

[3] Amortized Bayesian Prototype Meta-learning: A New Probabilistic Meta-learning Approach to Few-shot Image Classification, AISTAT 2021
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Overall this paper attacks a very important problem of meta learning and few-shot learning. And considering the quantitative results, the proposed method does achieve better uncertainty calibration. 
I expect further discussion on justifying the necessity of adopting an additional set encoder to achieve this capability.

reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.

reviews.text:
## Summary Of The Paper
The authors explore the uncertainty calibration problem in meta-learning few-shot classification, which is a relatively new topic that was seldom discussed before. 
To solve the challenges that the few samples in few-shot learning are usually insufficient for providing reliable estimation to the covariance matrices, the authors introduce meta-learning covariance matrices parametrized by a set encoder (implemented as set-transformer). And the authors further introduce scaled energy to parameterize a logit-normal softmax distribution for improving the uncertainty calibration of the softmax classification layer.

## Main Review
### Strengths
The overall presentation of this paper is both clear and straightforward.

The figures and equations precisely facilitate the reading. 

No noticeable typos were spotted. 

The topic that the authors selected is important and largely overlooked by the research of meta-learning and few-shot learning. The effectiveness of the proposed method is reflected by the quantitative comparisons. 

### Weakness
My major concern to this paper mainly arises from the use of an additional meta-learned set encoder to predict the task-specific covariance. 
It has been shown in previous work that some meta-learners have natural advantages in quantifying uncertainty. 

For example, relying on kernel methods, Gaussian processes based meta-learners [1,2] have demonstrated strong uncertainty calibration without leveraging any auxiliary components. 

Additionally, I might be wrong, but I'm curious that if this class-level covariance can be obtained through aggregating sample-wise information so that an additional set encoder can be removed. 
Please refer to Eq.6 to Eq. 9 of [3] for an example. Although the motivations are different, I believe there is some shared wisdom.

The authors propose a method to meta-learn class-wise covariance matrices that can be transferable across the task distribution. 
Specifically, they meta-learn a function that takes a set of class examples as an input and outputs a class-specific covariance matrix which is in the form of either a diagonal or diagonal plus low-rank factors. 
By doing so, the resulting covariance matrices remain expressive even with limited amounts of data. 
Further, in order to tackle the limitation caused by the shift invariance property of the softmax function, they propose to use scaled energy to parameterize a logit-normal softmax distribution which leads to better calibrated softmax scores.

## Expanded Review

The paper proposes a novel approach to meta-learning few-shot classification by predicting task-specific covariance matrices using a set encoder. 
The authors argue that existing approaches fail to generalize to the meta-learning setting, where there is a distribution over tasks. 
They propose to meta-learn a generative classifier that can estimate the class-wise distribution of query examples, conditioned on the support set. 
The generative classifier is a natural choice in this setting due to the fact that it utilizes feature space densities, which has been shown to be a requirement for accurate epistemic uncertainty prediction.

One of the key contributions of the paper is the proposal of a meta-learning framework that predicts diagonal or low-rank covariance factors as a function of a support set. 
This approach allows for efficient construction of task-specific covariance matrices, which is a crucial component of the proposed method. 
The authors also propose an energy-based inference procedure that leads to better calibrated uncertainty on out-of-distribution (OOD) data.

The authors provide a thorough evaluation of their proposed method on various benchmarks, including Omniglot and MiniImageNet. 
The results show that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches in terms of expected calibration error (ECE) and accuracy. 
The authors also provide a detailed analysis of the eigenvalue distribution of the predicted precision matrix, which shows that the proposed method is able to learn a class-specific, non-spherical Gaussian distribution.

## Additional Comments
The authors' use of a set encoder to predict task-specific covariance matrices is a key aspect of their approach. 
However, as noted in the original review, it is unclear whether this additional component is necessary. 
One potential direction for future work is to explore whether the class-level covariance can be obtained through aggregating sample-wise information, thereby removing the need for an additional set encoder.

## Conclusion
Overall, the paper presents a novel approach to meta-learning few-shot classification by predicting task-specific covariance matrices using a set encoder. 
The proposed method shows promising results on various benchmarks and provides a thorough analysis of the eigenvalue distribution of the predicted precision matrix. 
However, further discussion is needed to justify the necessity of adopting an additional set encoder to achieve this capability.

## Rating
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors explore the uncertainty calibration problem in meta-learning few-shot classification, which is a relatively new topic that was seldom discussed before. 
To solve the challenges that the few samples in few-shot learning are usually insufficient for providing reliable estimation to the covariance matrices, the authors introduce meta-learning covariance matrices parametrized by a set encoder (implemented as set-transformer). And the authors further introduce scaled energy to parameterize a logit-normal softmax distribution for improving the uncertainty calibration of the softmax classification layer.

Main Review:
Strengths:
The overall presentation of this paper is both clear and straightforward.

The figures and equations precisely facilitate the reading. 

No noticeable typos were spotted. 

The topic that the authors selected is important and largely overlooked by the research of meta-learning and few-shot learning. The effectiveness of the proposed method is reflected by the quantitative comparisons. 

Weakness:
My major concern to this paper mainly arises from the use of an additional meta-learned set encoder to predict the task-specific covariance. 
It has been shown in previous work that some meta-learners have natural advantages in quantifying uncertainty. 

For example, relying on kernel methods, Gaussian processes based meta-learners [1,2] have demonstrated strong uncertainty calibration without leveraging any auxiliary components. 

Additionally, I might be wrong, but I'm curious that if this class-level covariance can be obtained through aggregating sample-wise information so that an additional set encoder can be removed. 
Please refer to Eq.6 to Eq. 9 of [3] for an example. Although the motivations are different, I believe there is some shared wisdom. 

[1] Deep kernel transfer in gaussian processes for few-shot learning, NeurIPS 2020

[2] Bayesian Few-Shot Classiﬁcation with One-vs-Each Pólya-Gamma Augmented Gaussian Processes, ICLR 2021

[3] Amortized Bayesian Prototype Meta-learning: A New Probabilistic Meta-learning Approach to Few-shot Image Classification, AISTAT 2021

Summary Of The Review:
Overall this paper attacks a very important problem of meta learning and few-shot learning. And considering the quantitative results, the proposed method does achieve better uncertainty calibration. 
I expect further discussion on justifying the necessity of adopting an additional set encoder to achieve this capability.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors proposed methodologies to estimate the covariance matrix of embedded features under the meta-learning setting. The authors first showed that the empirical covariance matrix is inappropriate in this setting and that due to the shift invariant property of softmax function, ProtoNet could produce overly confident predictions. 

The authors instead propose to learn the covariance matrix through a Set Transformer structure, where the covariance matrix can be decomposed into the sum of a diagonal component and a low rank component. The authors used scaled energy and bi-Lipschitz regularization to make the methodology more performant. 

Numerical studies are performed to evaluate the calibration performance as well as the eigenvalue distribution of several methods.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is well written and easy to follow. Some specific comments / suggestions:
1. It would be great if the authors could expand the discussion of bi-Lipschitz regularization in the related work section. Some discussion and literature review of set-input functions, e.g., Set Transformer, Deep Sets will also be helpful.
2. What are the practical ways to ensure that Lambda_c matrix is non-negative, without making too many entries of Lambda_c to be zero?
3. Is there any benchmark on the computational requirement of the proposed methods, compared to competing methods? The covariance inversion and log-determinant will add computational cost to the methods, and it would be good to add some analysis to show the tradeoff between computational cost (i.e., number of iterations) versus performance.
4. In the numerical studies, it seems that the proposal performed comparatively better in the low-way low-shot setting. Is this the correct observation? If so, is there any intuition behind this?
